content:
# Genesis Chapter 50 — EMET (Literal) Translation
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 29r–29v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_050_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_050_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE | **LXX Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_050_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/GEN_050_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`
**View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0054 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0055

---

*Joseph Weeps Over His Father — The Embalming (vv. 1–3)*

**50:1** And Joseph [וְיוֹסֵף] fell [נָפַל] upon his father's face [עַל-פְּנֵי אָבִיו] and wept over him [וַיֵּבְךְּ עָלָיו] and kissed him [וַיִּשַּׁק-לוֹ].

**50:2** And Joseph commanded [וַיְצַו יוֹסֵף] his servants the physicians [אֶת-עֲבָדָיו אֶת-הָרֹפְאִים] to embalm [לַחֲנֹט] his father [אֶת-אָבִיו] — and the physicians embalmed [וַיַּחַנְטוּ הָרֹפְאִים] Israel [אֶת-יִשְׂרָאֵל].

**50:3** And forty days [וַיִּמְלְאוּ-לוֹ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם] were fulfilled for him — for so many days [כִּי כֵּן יִמְלְאוּ] are fulfilled the days of the embalmed [יְמֵי הַחֲנֻטִים] — and Egypt wept for him [וַיִּבְכּוּ אֹתוֹ מִצְרַיִם] seventy days [שִׁבְעִים יוֹם].

---

*Joseph Petitions Pharaoh — Permission to Bury in Canaan (vv. 4–6)*

**50:4** And when the days of his weeping had passed [וַיַּעַבְרוּ יְמֵי בְכִיתוֹ] — Joseph spoke [וַיְדַבֵּר יוֹסֵף] to the household of Pharaoh [אֶל-בֵּית פַּרְעֹה] saying [לֵאמֹר]: If now I have found favor [אִם-נָא מָצָאתִי חֵן] in your eyes [בְּעֵינֵיכֶם] — speak now [דַּבְּרוּ-נָא] in the ears of Pharaoh [בְּאָזְנֵי פַרְעֹה] saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**50:5** My father made me swear [אָבִי הִשְׁבִּיעַנִי] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Behold [הִנֵּה] — I am dying [אָנֹכִי מֵת] — in my grave [בְּקִבְרִי] which I dug for myself [אֲשֶׁר כָּרִיתִי לִי] in the land of Canaan [בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן] — there you shall bury me [שָׁמָּה תִּקְבְּרֵנִי] — and now [וְעַתָּה] let me go up now [אֶעֱלֶה-נָּא] and bury my father [וְאֶקְבְּרָה אֶת-אָבִי] — and I will return [וְשַׁבְתִּי].

**50:6** And Pharaoh said [וַיֹּאמֶר פַּרְעֹה]: Go up [עֲלֵה] and bury your father [וּקְבֹר אֶת-אָבִיךָ] as he made you swear [כַּאֲשֶׁר הִשְׁבִּיעֶךָ].

---

*The Great Funeral Procession to Canaan (vv. 7–14)*

**50:7** And Joseph went up [וַיַּעַל יוֹסֵף] to bury his father [לִקְבֹּר אֶת-אָבִיו] — and with him went up [וַיַּעֲלוּ אִתּוֹ] all the servants of Pharaoh [כָּל-עַבְדֵי פַרְעֹה] — the elders of his household [זִקְנֵי בֵיתוֹ] — and all the elders of the land of Egypt [וְכֹל זִקְנֵי אֶרֶץ-מִצְרָיִם].

**50:8** And all the household of Joseph [וְכֹל בֵּית יוֹסֵף] and his brothers [וְאֶחָיו] and his father's household [וּבֵית אָבִיו] — only their little ones [רַק טַפָּם] and their flocks [וְצֹאנָם] and their cattle [וּבְקָרָם] did they leave [עָזָבוּ] in the land of Goshen [בְּאֶרֶץ גֹּשֶׁן].

**50:9** And there went up with him [וַיַּעַל עִמּוֹ] both chariots [גַּם-רֶכֶב] and horsemen [גַּם-פָּרָשִׁים] — and it was a very great company [וַיְהִי הַמַּחֲנֶה כָּבֵד מְאֹד].

**50:10** And they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] to the threshing floor of Atad [עַד-גֹּרֶן הָאָטָד] which is beyond the Jordan [אֲשֶׁר בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן] — and they mourned there [וַיִּסְפְּדוּ-שָׁם] with a great and very heavy lamentation [מִסְפֵּד גָּדוֹל וְכָבֵד מְאֹד] — and he observed for his father [וַיַּעַשׂ לְאָבִיו] seven days of mourning [אֵבֶל שִׁבְעַת יָמִים].

**50:11** And when the inhabitants of the land saw [וַיַּרְא יוֹשֵׁב הָאָרֶץ] — the Canaanites [הַכְּנַעֲנִי] — the mourning [אֶת-הָאֵבֶל] at the threshing floor of Atad [בְּגֹרֶן הָאָטָד] — they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: This is a grievous mourning [אֵבֶל-כָּבֵד זֶה] to Egypt [לְמִצְרָיִם] — therefore its name was called [עַל-כֵּן קָרָא שְׁמָהּ] Abel-Mizraim [אָבֵל מִצְרַיִם] which is beyond the Jordan [אֲשֶׁר בְּעֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן].

**50:12** And his sons did for him [וַיַּעֲשׂוּ בָנָיו לוֹ] as he had commanded them [כֵּן כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּם].

**50:13** And his sons carried him [וַיִּשְׂאוּ אֹתוֹ בָנָיו] to the land of Canaan [אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן] and buried him [וַיִּקְבְּרוּ אֹתוֹ] in the cave of the field of Machpelah [בִּמְעָרַת שְׂדֵה הַמַּכְפֵּלָה] which Abraham bought [אֲשֶׁר קָנָה אַבְרָהָם] with the field [אֶת-הַשָּׂדֶה] for a burial possession [לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר] from Ephron the Hittite [מֵאֵת עֶפְרֹן הַחִתִּי] before Mamre [עַל-פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא].

**50:14** And Joseph returned [וַיָּשָׁב יוֹסֵף] to Egypt [מִצְרָיְמָה] — he [הוּא] and his brothers [וְאֶחָיו] and all who had gone up with him [וְכָל-הָעֹלִים אִתּוֹ] to bury his father [לִקְבֹּר אֶת-אָבִיו] — after he had buried his father [אַחֲרֵי קָבְרוֹ אֶת-אָבִיו].

---

*The Brothers' Fear — Joseph's Forgiveness Confirmed (vv. 15–21)*

**50:15** And the brothers of Joseph saw [וַיִּרְאוּ אֲחֵי-יוֹסֵף] that their father was dead [כִּי-מֵת אֲבִיהֶם] — and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: What if Joseph will hate us [לוּ יִשְׂטְמֵנוּ יוֹסֵף] — and will surely repay us [וְהָשֵׁב יָשִׁיב לָנוּ] all the evil [אֵת כָּל-הָרָעָה] that we dealt to him [אֲשֶׁר גָּמַלְנוּ אֹתוֹ]?

**50:16** And they commanded [וַיְצַוּוּ] to Joseph [אֶל-יוֹסֵף] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Your father commanded before he died [אָבִיךָ צִוָּה לִפְנֵי מוֹתוֹ] saying [לֵאמֹר]:

**50:17** Thus you shall say to Joseph [כֹּה-תֹאמְרוּ לְיוֹסֵף]: Please forgive now [אָנָּא שָׂא-נָא] the transgression [פֶּשַׁע] of your brothers [אַחֶיךָ] and their sin [וְחַטָּאתָם] — for they did evil to you [כִּי-רָעָה גְמָלוּךָ] — and now please forgive [וְעַתָּה שָׂא-נָא] the transgression [לְפֶשַׁע] of the servants of the God of your father [עַבְדֵי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ] — and Joseph wept [וַיֵּבְךְּ יוֹסֵף] when they spoke to him [בְּדַבְּרָם אֵלָיו].

**50:18** And his brothers also came [וַיֵּלְכוּ גַם-אֶחָיו] and fell down before him [וַיִּפְּלוּ לְפָנָיו] and said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Behold [הִנֶּנּוּ] — we are your servants [לְךָ לַעֲבָדִים].

**50:19** And Joseph said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יוֹסֵף]: Do not be afraid [אַל-תִּירָאוּ] — for am I in the place of God [כִּי הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנִי]?

**50:20** And you [וְאַתֶּם] — you intended evil against me [חֲשַׁבְתֶּם עָלַי רָעָה] — but God [אֱלֹהִים] intended it for good [חֲשָׁבָהּ לְטֹבָה] — in order to bring about [לְמַעַן עֲשׂוֹת] as it is this day [כַּיּוֹם הַזֶּה] — to preserve alive a great people [לְהַחֲיֹת עַם-רָב].

**50:21** And now [וְעַתָּה] — do not be afraid [אַל-תִּירָאוּ] — I myself will provide for you [אָנֹכִי אֲכַלְכֵּל אֶתְכֶם] and your little ones [וְאֶת-טַפְּכֶם] — and he comforted them [וַיְנַחֵם אוֹתָם] and spoke to their heart [וַיְדַבֵּר עַל-לִבָּם].

---

*Joseph Lives to See the Third Generation — His Final Request (vv. 22–25)*

**50:22** And Joseph dwelt [וַיֵּשֶׁב יוֹסֵף] in Egypt [בְּמִצְרַיִם] — he [הוּא] and his father's household [וּבֵית אָבִיו] — and Joseph lived [וַיְחִי יוֹסֵף] a hundred and ten years [מֵאָה וָעֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים].

**50:23** And Joseph saw [וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף] Ephraim's children [לְאֶפְרַיִם] of the third generation [בְּנֵי שִׁלֵּשִׁים] — also the children of Machir [גַּם בְּנֵי מָכִיר] the son of Manasseh [בֶּן-מְנַשֶּׁה] were born [יֻלְּדוּ] upon the knees of Joseph [עַל-בִּרְכֵּי יוֹסֵף].

**50:24** And Joseph said [וַיֹּאמֶר יוֹסֵף] to his brothers [אֶל-אֶחָיו]: I am dying [אָנֹכִי מֵת] — but God will surely visit you [וֵאלֹהִים פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֶתְכֶם] — and will bring you up [וְהֶעֱלָה אֶתְכֶם] out of this land [מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת] to the land [אֶל-הָאָרֶץ] that he swore [אֲשֶׁר נִשְׁבַּע] to Abraham [לְאַבְרָהָם] — to Isaac [לְיִצְחָק] — and to Jacob [וּלְיַעֲקֹב].

**50:25** And Joseph adjured [וַיַּשְׁבַּע יוֹסֵף] the sons of Israel [אֶת-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] saying [לֵאמֹר]: God will surely visit you [פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶתְכֶם] — and you shall carry up my bones [וְהַעֲלִיתֶם אֶת-עַצְמֹתַי] from here [מִזֶּה].

---

*The Death of Joseph — Genesis Ends with a Coffin in Egypt (v. 26)*

**50:26** And Joseph died [וַיָּמָת יוֹסֵף] the son of a hundred and ten years [בֶּן-מֵאָה וָעֶשֶׂר שָׁנִים] — and they embalmed him [וַיַּחַנְטוּ אֹתוֹ] — and he was placed [וַיִּישֶׂם] in a coffin [בָּאָרוֹן] in Egypt [בְּמִצְרָיִם].

---

## Translation Notes

### Verse-by-Verse Key Decisions

**v. 1 — "fell upon his father's face"**
Hebrew: וַיִּפֹּל יוֹסֵף עַל-פְּנֵי אָבִיו. The phrase "fell upon the face" is a literal rendering of a gesture of prostrate grief — the body thrown forward over the face of the deceased. EMET preserves this idiom rather than softening to "threw himself upon."

**v. 2 — "his servants the physicians" / "embalm"**
The hapax construct עֲבָדָיו אֶת-הָרֹפְאִים is rendered literally: the physicians are a subset of the servants. חָנַט (chanat) = to embalm; this is an Egyptian custom, not a Hebrew one — the text preserves the cultural foreignness.

**v. 3 — "forty days ... seventy days"**
The text distinguishes the period of embalming completion (forty days) from the national mourning period of Egypt (seventy days). These are held distinct in the translation. The Egyptians mourned Jacob almost as they mourned a pharaoh (the full mourning period for a pharaoh was seventy-two days; seventy days here is a remarkable honor).

**v. 4 — "household of Pharaoh" not "to Pharaoh"**
Joseph did not approach Pharaoh directly — he was in mourning and unclean, unable by Egyptian custom to appear before the king. He used intermediaries. The text says בֵּית פַּרְעֹה (house/household of Pharaoh), and this distinction is preserved literally.

**v. 5 — "my grave which I dug for myself"**
כָּרִיתִי (kariti) from כָּרָה = to dig. Some translations render this "which I prepared," but the Hebrew is concrete: Jacob dug it. The MT preserves the specificity; EMET retains it.

**v. 10 — "threshing floor of Atad" (גֹּרֶן הָאָטָד)**
Atad (הָאָטָד) is a proper noun meaning "the bramble/thornbush." The place-name is preserved as a proper noun in line with the literal approach. "Beyond the Jordan" — the phrase עֵבֶר הַיַּרְדֵּן indicates the Transjordanian side (east bank), which has been noted as geographically puzzling for a procession from Egypt to Canaan; the text is rendered as-is without harmonization.

**v. 11 — "Abel-Mizraim" (אָבֵל מִצְרַיִם)**
The place-name preserves the Hebrew wordplay: אָבֵל can mean either "meadow/stream" or "mourning" (from אָבַל = to mourn). The text plays on "mourning of Egypt." The name is transliterated rather than translated, consistent with project conventions.

**v. 13 — "burial possession" (אֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר)**
The construct chain is preserved: "possession of burial" = a burial holding. This echoes the language of Gen 23 (the Machpelah purchase) and ties the legal terminology together across the narrative.

**v. 15 — "What if Joseph will hate us" (לוּ יִשְׂטְמֵנוּ יוֹסֵף)**
לוּ here introduces a feared hypothetical: "what if" / "perhaps." שָׂטַם = to bear a grudge, to hate persistently (same root as "Satan" in some uses). "Hate" is preferable to the weak "resent." The verb וְהָשֵׁב יָשִׁיב (infinitive absolute + imperfect) = "will surely repay" — this doubling is preserved.

**v. 17 — "transgression" (פֶּשַׁע)**
פֶּשַׁע (pesha) = willful transgression, a deliberate breach of relationship — the strongest Hebrew term for sin in the triad (pesha / avon / chata'ah). It is distinct from חַטָּאתָם (chatta'tam = their sin, missing the mark). Both terms appear in v. 17 and are rendered distinctly: "transgression" and "sin." The differentiation signals the brothers' own awareness of the gravity of what they did: they did not merely err — they rebelled.

**v. 19 — "Am I in the place of God?"**
Hebrew: הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנִי (ha-tachat Elohim ani). Word order: the interrogative particle + "under/in place of" + "God" + "I." The word order in Hebrew puts "God" before "I," which is theologically significant. Rendered as literally as English syntax allows: "for am I in the place of God?" — the "I" at the end emphasizes the audacity of any human who would claim such a position.

**v. 20 — The Central Theological Statement**
אַתֶּם חֲשַׁבְתֶּם עָלַי רָעָה — אֱלֹהִים חֲשָׁבָהּ לְטֹבָה

The verb חָשַׁב (chashav) appears twice: first with the brothers as subject (chashavtem = you intended/reckoned/devised), then with God as subject (chashavah = he intended/reckoned/devised). The same verb, two subjects, opposite objects (ra'ah = evil; tovah = good). This is the Joseph narrative's capstone: God's providential reckoning works through — not around — human wickedness.

"You intended evil against me" — not "you meant evil to me" (the preposition עָלַי = against me, with a directional force).
"God intended it for good" — לְטֹבָה, the lamed of purpose or direction: toward good.

לְהַחֲיֹת עַם-רָב = "to preserve alive a great people" — the Hiphil infinitive of חָיָה = to cause to live, to keep alive. עַם-רָב = a great/numerous people. This is the redemptive scope: not merely the family of Jacob but "a great people" — anticipating the nation of Israel and the nations fed through Joseph's administration.

**v. 24 — "God will surely visit you" (פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד)**
The infinitive absolute + finite verb construction (paqod yifqod) intensifies and assures: "visiting he will visit" = "will surely visit." The verb פָּקַד (paqad) = to visit, attend to, muster, appoint — a rich word carrying the sense of divine attention that changes a situation. It is rendered "will surely visit" rather than "will take care of you" or "will come to your aid" — the strangeness of "visit" is preserved because the word recurs at the Exodus as a technical theological term (Exod 3:16; 4:31). Preserving it here creates the bridge the narrative intends.

**v. 25 — "you shall carry up my bones" (וְהַעֲלִיתֶם אֶת-עַצְמֹתַי)**
The verb עָלָה (alah) = to go up, carry up. The "up" is significant: up out of Egypt, up toward the land, up as a theological ascent. "Carry up" (not "take") preserves the directional force. This oath is the bridge to Exod 13:19, where Moses fulfills it, and to Josh 24:32, where it is finally accomplished at Shechem. The bones of Joseph span four centuries and three books of the Torah.

**v. 26 — "coffin" (אָרוֹן)**
אָרוֹן appears elsewhere as "ark" — the Ark of Noah, the Ark of the Covenant. Here, in the only other use in Genesis, it is a "coffin." The word is not softened to "sarcophagus" or "chest." It is a coffin. The final word of Genesis is בְּמִצְרָיִם — "in Egypt." Genesis ends with this prepositional phrase: in Egypt. Not in Canaan, not in the land of promise — in Egypt. The coffin of Joseph is the unresolved tension that Genesis leaves to be resolved by Exodus.

---

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus vs. MT)

**v. 3 — Days of mourning**
The LXX (Vaticanus) renders the forty days of embalming and seventy days of mourning consistently with the MT. No significant divergence here.

**v. 5 — "my grave which I dug"**
LXX: ἐν τῷ μνήματί μου ᾧ ὤρυξα (en to mnemati mou ho oryksa) = "in my tomb which I dug" — supporting the MT's concrete verb כָּרִיתִי. Some later versions spiritualize this; Vaticanus is literal.

**v. 10 — "threshing floor of Atad"**
LXX: ἅλω Ἀτάδ (halo Atad) = "threshing floor of Atad" — consistent with MT. The LXX adds a note clarifying the location as "beyond the Jordan," matching MT.

**v. 11 — "Abel-Mizraim"**
LXX: Πένθος Αἰγύπτου (Penthos Aigyptou) = "Mourning of Egypt" — the LXX translates the Hebrew wordplay rather than transliterating the place-name. This is a significant divergence from the MT transliteration. The MT's אָבֵל מִצְרַיִם preserves the ambiguity between "meadow of Egypt" and "mourning of Egypt"; the LXX opts for "mourning," interpreting the etymology for the reader.

**v. 20 — The theological center**
LXX: ὑμεῖς ἐβουλεύσασθε κατ' ἐμοῦ εἰς πονηρά, ὁ δὲ θεὸς ἐβουλεύσατο περὶ ἐμοῦ εἰς ἀγαθά (hymeis ebouleusasthe kat' emou eis ponera, ho de theos ebouleusato peri emou eis agatha). The LXX uses βουλεύομαι (to deliberate, plan, counsel) for both subjects — a strong word capturing the intentional, deliberate nature of both the evil scheme and God's counter-purpose. The LXX's περὶ ἐμοῦ ("concerning me") vs. the MT's עָלַי ("against me" for the brothers) softens slightly the directional force of the brothers' malice in the second clause, but the overall theological thrust is identical.

**v. 23 — "third generation"**
LXX: ἕως τρίτης γενεᾶς (heos trites geneas) = "unto the third generation" — consistent with MT's בְּנֵי שִׁלֵּשִׁים.

**v. 24 — "God will surely visit you"**
LXX: ἐπισκοπῇ ἐπισκέψεται ὑμᾶς ὁ θεός (episkope episkepsetai hymas ho theos) — the LXX renders the infinitive absolute with a cognate noun construction ("with a visiting he will visit you"), capturing the force of the doubled Hebrew verb. This is one of the more elegant LXX equivalents to the Hebrew rhetorical device.

**v. 26 — "coffin"**
LXX: σορός (soros) = a funerary urn, ossuary, or coffin. The LXX uses the standard Greek term for a receptacle of remains. The MT's אָרוֹן, which carries the conceptual echo of "ark," is flattened in the Greek — the resonance with the Ark of the Covenant is lost in the LXX rendering.

---

## Closing Note: Genesis Ends with a Coffin

Genesis is a book about beginnings — of the cosmos, of humanity, of sin, of the family of Abraham. But it does not end with a triumph. It ends with a coffin.

וַיִּישֶׂם בָּאָרוֹן בְּמִצְרָיִם — "and he was placed in a coffin in Egypt."

Three words at the end: coffin, Egypt. The land of promise is off-screen. The covenant made to Abraham, reaffirmed to Isaac, wrestled for at Jabbok — none of it has been inherited. Jacob has been buried in Canaan, yes, but his body had to be brought out and brought back again. Joseph's bones will wait. The great people that God intends to preserve alive is not yet a nation; it is a family of seventy that went down to Egypt in Genesis 46, and now the man who saved them is dead and embalmed and in a box.

The verb יִישֶׂם (yissem) — "was placed" — is the passive form of שִׂים (sim) = to put, place, set. Joseph, who placed his hands on the knees of his grandchildren (v. 23), who spoke the promise of divine visitation (vv. 24–25), is himself placed. He is still. The story is not.

Joseph's oath in verse 25 — פָּקֹד יִפְקֹד אֱלֹהִים אֶתְכֶם ("God will surely visit you") — is the unresolved note on which Genesis closes. It is promise-as-demand, hope-as-obligation. The bones must be carried up. The story must continue. Exodus opens precisely where Genesis ends: in Egypt, multiplying, under the shadow of a forgotten Joseph (Exod 1:6–8).

The structural bookend is exact: Genesis opens with God creating out of formless void (1:2); Genesis ends with Joseph's body placed in a box in a foreign land. Creation and death, beginning and waiting. The entire Pentateuch is stretched between these poles — from the breath of life breathed into Adam's nostrils to the bones of Joseph waiting in an Egyptian coffin for the moment when God visits his people and the exodus begins.

The final chapter of Genesis is not a conclusion. It is a pause in a promise.
